name: The First National Bank of Granbury
description_short: FDIC-insured community bank chartered in 1887, offering consumer checking, savings, mortgages, and commercial lending with a focus on local relationship banking.
description_long: First National Bank of Granbury was established in 1887 by Texas pioneers Dan Cogdell, John Traylor, E.A. Hannaford, Jess Baker, and James H. Doyle. The bank has operated continuously for over 136 years, including through the Great Depression and droughts of the 1930s, when it absorbed accounts from failing City National Bank to protect community savings. During WWII and the 1960s, bank leadership played active roles in major civic projects including the War Bond Drive and the damming of the Brazos River to form Lake Granbury. FNB offers a comprehensive suite of consumer and business banking products including checking accounts, savings accounts, money market accounts, certificates of deposit (CDs), consumer loans, commercial loans, mortgage products, and online loan payment processing. Customers can open accounts online and apply for home loans through their Mortgage Advisors. The bank provides online banking access, secure email communication, debit cards, and e-statements. They are FDIC-insured and backed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. Government. The bank's primary distinction is its explicit commitment to community banking relationship-building rather than transactional convenience. Their mission statement—"We treat people the way we want to be treated"—reflects a philosophy that customers are individuals with whom bankers build relationships, not merely account holders. The bank emphasizes that their employees want to know customers personally and that their service extends beyond their physical locations into the broader community. FNB is best suited for customers in the Granbury area who value personalized service and community connection over digital-first banking experiences. As a traditional community bank, they likely have limited geographic reach and fewer technological innovations compared to larger regional or national banks. Prospective customers should verify current branch locations, product availability, and competitive rates before opening accounts.
services: Debit cards with lost/stolen card replacement
pros: 136+ years of continuous operation and community presence, survived Great Depression and major economic downturns
FDIC-insured deposits backed by full faith and credit of U.S. Government
Mission-driven community banking model emphasizing personal relationships rather than transactional service
Comprehensive product suite including consumer checking/savings, CDs, mortgages, consumer loans, and commercial lending
Online account opening for consumer products and online loan payment processing available
Professional mortgage advisors available for home purchase and refinance assistance
Multiple community leadership initiatives documented (War Bond Drive, Lake Granbury development, historic square renovation)
cons: Limited geographic footprint as a local community bank, not accessible to customers outside Granbury area
Website lacks detailed information on current interest rates, fees, minimum balances, or product terms
No mention of mobile banking app, mobile check deposit, or modern digital banking conveniences
Limited product innovation; no mention of credit cards, investment services, or wealth management products
Smaller institution means fewer ATM locations and less extensive branch network compared to regional/national banks
best_for: Granbury-area residents seeking relationship-based community banking with personal service
Small business owners and commercial borrowers in Hood County requiring local lending relationships
Homebuyers in the Granbury area working with mortgage advisors for purchase or refinance
Customers who prioritize personal banker relationships and community involvement over digital-first banking
diagnosis: First National Bank of Granbury is best for customers in or near Granbury, Texas who value personalized relationship banking and community involvement over digital convenience and competitive rates. The main caveat is that as a small community bank with 136+ years of traditional operations, they likely lack the technological sophistication, geographic accessibility, and competitive product terms of larger regional or national banks.
typical_results_timeline: Account opening typically takes 1-3 business days. Loan decisions vary by product.
